subtopics.title: What's in the Box
subtopics.content: You need one checkerboard, which is an eight-by-eight grid of alternating light and dark squares. This is the same board used for chess. You also need twelve dark checkers for one player and twelve light checkers for the other, giving you twenty-four pieces total.

subtopics.title: Board Layout
subtopics.content: Place the board so that each player has a dark square in their lower-left corner. Every move in checkers happens on the dark squares only. The light squares are never used at all. There are thirty-two dark squares on the board, and they form a diagonal pattern that your pieces will travel along for the entire game.

subtopics.title: Player Setup
subtopics.content: Each player fills the dark squares of the three rows closest to them with their twelve checkers. That uses up twelve dark squares per side and leaves the two middle rows completely empty. These empty rows are the battlefield where the action begins. Every piece starts the game as a regular man, placed flat on the board. Kings do not exist at the start and are only created during play.

subtopics.title: Starting the Game
subtopics.content: The player with the dark-colored pieces always moves first, and then players alternate turns back and forth. In tournament play, players swap colors between games so that neither side always gets the advantage of moving first.

subtopics.title: Turn Structure
subtopics.content: On each turn you move exactly one piece. Regular men move diagonally forward one square onto an empty dark square. If a jump is available anywhere on the board, you must take it instead of making a regular move. When you jump, your piece leaps diagonally over an adjacent opponent piece and lands on the empty square beyond it, capturing that opponent piece. If your piece lands and can immediately jump again, you must keep jumping in the same turn. A single turn can chain together several jumps this way. You are never allowed to skip your turn.

subtopics.title: Actions & Options
subtopics.content:
Regular men are your basic pieces. They move diagonally forward one square at a time, and they capture by jumping diagonally forward over an opponent piece to the empty square just beyond it. Men can only move and capture in the forward direction, which means toward your opponent's side of the board.

When one of your men reaches the farthest row on the opposite side of the board, it becomes a king. You crown it by stacking a second checker on top or flipping it over, depending on your set. Kings are significantly more powerful than men because they can move and capture diagonally in all four directions, both forward and backward. This makes kings excellent at chasing down opponents and controlling the board. A single king can often outmaneuver two or three regular men because of this freedom of movement.

subtopics.title: Special Rules
subtopics.content:
The forced capture rule is one of the most important rules in checkers. If any of your pieces can make a jump on your turn, you must jump. You are not allowed to make a regular non-jumping move when a capture is available. If you have more than one piece that can jump, or more than one direction to jump, you get to choose which jump to take, but you must take one of them.

Kinging during a multi-jump chain has a special rule. If a man reaches the king row in the middle of a chain of jumps, it is crowned immediately and its turn ends right there. The piece does not continue jumping even if additional jumps would have been available. It will move as a king starting on its next turn.

Capture removal timing also matters. Each jumped piece is removed from the board immediately after that individual jump, not after the whole chain finishes. This is important because removing a piece during a chain can open up new squares or block squares that affect whether the remaining jumps in the chain are possible.

subtopics.title: Endgame & Scoring
subtopics.content:
You win the game by capturing all twelve of your opponent's pieces or by leaving your opponent with no legal moves on their turn. If all of their remaining pieces are blocked and cannot move or jump, you win immediately.

The game is a draw if both players agree to one. Under tournament rules, a draw is also declared if forty consecutive moves pass with no capture and no piece being crowned king. A draw can also happen when neither player has enough material or positional strength to force a win, such as when each player has just one king left and neither can trap the other.

subtopics.title: Opening Priorities
subtopics.content: In the opening, aim your pieces toward the center of the board. Pieces in the center have more diagonal options and more jump opportunities than pieces stuck on the edges. At the same time, keep your back row intact for as long as you can. Your back row is the last line of defense against your opponent crowning a king, and once those squares are empty your opponent can slip right through. A strong opening plan is to advance two or three pieces to the fourth and fifth rows while keeping solid support behind them so they are not easily picked off.

subtopics.title: Common Mistakes
subtopics.content:
The most common beginner mistake is emptying the back row too early. When you move all of your back-row pieces forward, you leave a wide-open path for your opponent to reach your king row and get a free king. Keep at least one or two defenders on that back row until you are ready to give them up on purpose.

Another frequent error is forgetting about forced captures. Because jumping is mandatory, your opponent can bait you into bad positions by placing a sacrifice piece where you are forced to take it. Before every move, ask yourself what jumps your opponent will be forced to make and look for traps you can set.

Beginners also tend to chase pieces deep into enemy territory with a single unsupported checker. An isolated piece is easy to corner and capture. Move your pieces in coordinated groups so they protect each other.

Finally, when you are ahead in kings, trade them one for one whenever possible. Each even trade increases your relative advantage. When you are behind, avoid trades and look for ways to crown another piece.

subtopics.title: Key Decisions
subtopics.content:
King strategy is at the heart of competitive checkers. Kings move in all four diagonal directions, making them dramatically stronger than men. Reaching the king row while your opponent has no kings is often a game-winning advantage. It is frequently worth sacrificing a regular man if it lets you crown a piece, because one king is often more valuable than two men in the late game.

Forced capture tactics are the bread and butter of checkers combinations. Because your opponent must jump whenever possible, you can offer a sacrifice piece that forces them to land on a square where you can jump two or even three of their pieces on your next turn. Learning to spot these two-for-one and three-for-one exchanges is the fastest way to improve.

In the endgame, kings rule the board. A single king against a single king is usually a draw because neither can force a capture. Two kings against one, however, is a win if you use them together to squeeze the lone king into a corner or along an edge. Never separate your kings in the endgame because their power comes from working as a team to cut off escape routes.

The opposition is a subtle but critical endgame concept. When two kings face each other on the same diagonal, the player who must move first is at a disadvantage because moving means giving ground. Sometimes the strongest play is the one that hands the move to your opponent and forces their king to retreat first.

metadata.notes: Content rewritten to production quality. American Checkers (8x8 board, forced captures). Source: ACF Official Rules. Redundancy between actions and special-mechanics resolved.
